Silvertop plans to reposition two buildings as co-working and flexible office space, convert one to residential through a 18-to-24-month entitlement process, and lease the remaining two as conventional office at $21 to $24 per square foot. Total planned capital expenditure is $28 million over three years. Redfern Bridge Capital provided $38 million in floating-rate financing at SOFR plus 290 basis points. Silvertop targets a 2.1x equity multiple and 12 to 14 percent unlevered IRR. Brockmore Office Advisors ran the competitive sale process.
restate_1: Silvertop Real Estate Capital is pleased to announce the closing of the Crestwick Office Portfolio acquisition through Silvertop Opportunity Fund III — a strategically differentiated suburban office repositioning program targeting the best risk-adjusted opportunity in the Brockmore market. The five-building, 610,000-square-foot portfolio was acquired at $95.41 per square foot from Clearmont Properties, whose post-pandemic occupancy challenges created an entry point that we believe significantly undervalues the portfolio's reuse and repositioning potential. Our multi-pronged business plan addresses each building individually: co-working and flexible office repositioning for two buildings, residential conversion for one subject to entitlement, and conventional office leasing at $21 to $24 per square foot for the remaining two. A $28 million capital investment over three years, financed in part by Redfern Bridge Capital's $38 million floating-rate facility, will transform this portfolio. Our target of a 2.1x equity multiple and 12 to 14 percent unlevered IRR reflects the conviction we have in this differentiated strategy.
restate_2: Silvertop Opportunity Fund III's $58.2 million acquisition of the Crestwick Office Portfolio carries a multi-pronged repositioning strategy that needs to be evaluated component by component. The co-working and flexible office conversion for two buildings faces a competitive market: national and regional co-working operators have shown selectivity in new location commitments, and suburban Brockmore may not be a priority expansion market for operators focused on urban cores. The residential conversion of one building through an 18-to-24-month entitlement process is the highest-risk element: entitlement timelines routinely extend beyond initial projections, and the cost of conversion from commercial to residential use frequently exceeds initial budgets. The conventional office leasing component at $21 to $24 per square foot in three Brockmore submarkets requires market rent validation — a 64 percent occupied starting point across five buildings suggests these submarkets have not been generating strong leasing velocity. The SOFR plus 290 basis point bridge from Redfern Bridge Capital is expensive for a transitional office platform. The 2.1x equity multiple target is achievable in a best-case scenario but optimistic across all three repositioning strategies simultaneously.
restate_3: Silvertop Opportunity Fund III's approach to the Crestwick Office Portfolio reflects the adaptive reuse investment thesis that has become standard for suburban office value-add capital: acquire distressed suburban office at a significant discount to replacement cost, implement a differentiated multi-strategy business plan, and create value through repositioning rather than conventional leasing. At $95.41 per square foot for 610,000 square feet of 64-percent-occupied suburban office in Brockmore, Silvertop is acquiring well below what any of the proposed repositioning uses would cost to develop from the ground up. The three-pronged business plan — co-working, residential conversion, and conventional office re-leasing — distributes risk across different demand categories, reducing reliance on any single repositioning outcome. Redfern Bridge Capital's $38 million floating-rate financing at SOFR plus 290 basis points provides execution capital at a price consistent with transitional suburban office bridge lending. Silvertop's 12 to 14 percent unlevered IRR target requires the successful execution of at least two of the three repositioning strategies — a reasonable probability in a diversified five-building portfolio.
restate_4: The Crestwick Office Portfolio acquisition by Silvertop Opportunity Fund III was a representative example of the suburban office value-add strategy that dominated distressed commercial real estate investment in 2026. Clearmont Properties' decision to exit the 64-percent-occupied, 610,000-square-foot portfolio at $95.41 per square foot — acknowledging the occupancy deterioration since pandemic-era lease expirations — created the entry point that Silvertop's opportunistic mandate required. The three-strategy repositioning program — co-working, residential conversion, and conventional office re-leasing — was executed with mixed results over the three-year investment horizon. The residential conversion entitlement took 26 months, two months beyond the initial estimate, but the converted building ultimately leased at rents above initial projections. The co-working repositioning attracted a regional operator to one building; the second remained in conventional leasing mode. The remaining two conventional office buildings leased to 78 percent occupancy at $22 per square foot. Final realized unlevered IRR was approximately 11.3 percent — at the lower bound of the 12 to 14 percent target — reflecting the execution complexity of a multi-strategy suburban office repositioning.

paragraph: Pelham Real Estate Debt Fund V originated a $95 million first-mortgage bridge loan secured by Meridian Station, a 402-unit multifamily community located at 2600 Station Boulevard in the Midtown submarket of Grenville. The borrower, Grenville Residential Partners, acquired the stabilized community for $139 million, or $345,771 per unit, from a closed-end opportunistic fund at a going-in cap rate of 5.3 percent. The loan carries a floating rate of SOFR plus 210 basis points, with a two-year initial term and two one-year extension options. Grenville Residential Partners intends to execute a $15 million capital improvement plan focused on amenity upgrades, unit interior renovations on 180 units, and exterior landscaping improvements. Average in-place rents at the time of acquisition were $1,820 per month, estimated at 8 percent below market. The property was delivered in 2017 and is currently 96.8 percent occupied. Pelham Real Estate Debt Fund V's loan represents a 68.3 percent loan-to-cost ratio. The Grenville multifamily market has sustained rent growth averaging 4.8 percent annually for the past three years, according to Meridian Apartment Analytics. Whitmore Capital Markets arranged the financing.

restate_1: Pelham Real Estate Debt Fund V announces the origination of a $95 million first-mortgage bridge loan secured by Meridian Station, a high-quality 402-unit multifamily community in the Midtown submarket of Grenville — a transaction that exemplifies Pelham's approach to disciplined, structured value-add bridge lending. Grenville Residential Partners' acquisition of the 2017-vintage community at $345,771 per unit, supported by a going-in cap rate of 5.3 percent and 96.8 percent occupancy, reflects a stabilized-plus strategy: capturing the 8 percent estimated below-market rent gap through a $15 million renovation program targeting amenities, 180 unit interiors, and exterior landscaping. Grenville's multifamily market's 4.8 percent annual rent growth, documented by Meridian Apartment Analytics, provides the fundamental demand backdrop that makes this renovation premium achievable. The SOFR plus 210 basis points floating rate and 68.3 percent loan-to-cost ratio reflect our conservative underwriting standards. We thank Whitmore Capital Markets for their arrangement expertise.
restate_2: Pelham Real Estate Debt Fund V's $95 million bridge loan on Meridian Station at 68.3 percent loan-to-cost is a well-structured transaction at a property level, but the all-in cost structure merits examination from Grenville Residential Partners' perspective. The borrower acquired Meridian Station for $139 million — a total capitalization with the $15 million renovation program of approximately $154 million. The SOFR plus 210 basis point floating loan on $95 million creates significant interest carry during the renovation and mark-to-market lease-up period. The $1,820 in-place rents estimated 8 percent below market implies a market rent of approximately $1,969 per month — achievable, but requiring successful renovation execution across 180 of 402 units while maintaining 96.8 percent occupancy. The 4.8 percent Grenville market rent growth is a supportive statistic, but the renovation premium assumption is incremental to market rent growth, not a substitute for it. At $345,771 per unit on a 2017-vintage asset in the Midtown submarket, the acquisition pricing is aggressive relative to stabilized comps in the submarket and requires the full renovation thesis to generate adequate returns.
restate_3: Pelham Real Estate Debt Fund V's Meridian Station origination reflects the well-established playbook for stabilized multifamily bridge lending: below-market rents on a high-occupancy, relatively new asset, supported by a documented submarket rent growth trend and a defined renovation capital program. The 68.3 percent loan-to-cost ratio on a $139 million acquisition plus $15 million renovation program provides adequate collateral coverage for a bridge lender. The 96.8 percent occupancy at origination minimizes vacancy risk during the renovation and reduces the operational uncertainty that undermines more distressed multifamily bridge situations. Grenville Residential Partners' acquisition of the 402-unit, 2017-vintage community at $345,771 per unit — from a closed-end opportunistic fund seeking liquidity at its fund maturity — suggests the borrower captured the asset at a price modestly below what a stabilized sale process might have generated. The SOFR plus 210 basis point floating rate, with two-year initial term and two one-year extensions, gives Pelham meaningful term flexibility to manage through any renovation or lease-up delays.
restate_4: The Meridian Station bridge financing was a transaction that fit neatly within the established value-add multifamily lending parameters that Pelham Real Estate Debt Fund V applied to the Grenville market. Grenville Residential Partners acquired the 402-unit, 2017-vintage community from a closed-end opportunistic fund that was approaching its investment horizon — a motivated seller dynamic that allowed acquisition at $345,771 per unit, slightly below the stabilized market comp set. The $95 million bridge at SOFR plus 210 basis points funded both the acquisition and the $15 million renovation program. The 180-unit interior renovation generated premiums averaging $155 per month — within the projected range — and was completed within 22 months of loan origination. Amenity upgrades and exterior landscaping were well-received by prospective tenants, supporting absorption of renovated units above the 8 percent below-market rent assumption. Grenville Residential Partners refinanced into Fandon Mae permanent debt at the end of the two-year initial term, repaying Pelham ahead of the first extension option. The 4.8 percent annual market rent growth in Grenville proved accurate, supporting the renovation premium thesis throughout the hold period.

paragraph: Trellis Commerce Trust, a specialty industrial REIT, completed the acquisition of Trellis Cold Storage Center, a 540,000-square-foot temperature-controlled warehouse facility in the Northhaven industrial submarket of Dunford, for $148.5 million, or $275 per square foot, reflecting the premium associated with specialized cold storage infrastructure. The facility, developed by Harwick Cold Chain Partners in 2021 at a cost of $118 million, includes 420,000 square feet of refrigerated storage across four temperature zones ranging from -20 degrees Fahrenheit to 38 degrees Fahrenheit, along with 120,000 square feet of dry warehouse and processing space. The property is 100 percent leased to Meridian Fresh Logistics under a 15-year triple-net lease, with two-percent annual rent escalations and a current annual rent of $8.4 million. The going-in cap rate was 5.65 percent. Financing consisted of a $95 million ten-year fixed-rate mortgage from Galveston Life Insurance at 5.55 percent. Trellis Commerce Trust estimates a replacement cost premium of more than 30 percent relative to the acquisition price, providing significant downside protection. Cold storage market vacancy in Dunford stood at 1.8 percent as of Q1 2026. Westport Industrial Advisors exclusively represented the seller.

Galveston Life Insurance provided a $95 million ten-year fixed-rate mortgage at 5.55 percent. Dunford's cold storage vacancy was 1.8 percent in Q1 2026. restate_1: Trellis Commerce Trust is pleased to announce the acquisition of the Trellis Cold Storage Center — a premier 540,000-square-foot temperature-controlled warehouse in Dunford's Northhaven industrial submarket — adding a highly specialized, income-generating asset to our specialty industrial platform. The facility's four refrigerated temperature zones, spanning -20 degrees Fahrenheit to 38 degrees Fahrenheit, represent infrastructure that is extremely difficult and capital-intensive to replicate, a point reinforced by our estimate that replacement cost exceeds our $148.5 million acquisition price by more than 30 percent. Meridian Fresh Logistics' 15-year triple-net lease, with 2 percent annual escalations and $8.4 million in current annual rent, provides exceptional long-term income certainty. The 5.65 percent going-in cap rate and Galveston Life Insurance's $95 million ten-year fixed-rate mortgage at 5.55 percent reflect strong institutional support for this transaction. Dunford's 1.8 percent cold storage vacancy underscores the scarcity of this specialized product in the market.
restate_2: Trellis Commerce Trust's $148.5 million acquisition of the Trellis Cold Storage Center requires a careful look at the single-tenant concentration risk embedded in the investment structure. Meridian Fresh Logistics occupies 100 percent of the 540,000-square-foot facility on a 15-year triple-net lease — a structure that provides excellent income predictability in a going-concern scenario but creates catastrophic vacancy exposure if Meridian Fresh Logistics encounters business difficulties. Cold storage is highly specialized: if Meridian vacates, finding a replacement tenant for 420,000 square feet of refrigerated space across four temperature zones is a time-consuming and capital-intensive process. The $275 per square foot acquisition price represents a $30.5 million premium over the 2021 development cost of $118 million — reasonable appreciation over five years but leaving less room for error than the replacement-cost narrative implies. The 5.65 percent going-in cap rate against a 5.55 percent fixed-rate mortgage produces only 10 basis points of positive leverage, meaning Trellis is essentially borrowing at the same cost as its going-in cap rate. The 2 percent annual rent escalation on $8.4 million of current rent provides modest but contractual income growth over the 15-year term.
restate_3: Trellis Commerce Trust's cold storage acquisition at $275 per square foot is a textbook example of the specialty industrial asset premium. Cold storage infrastructure — four temperature zones from -20 degrees Fahrenheit to 38 degrees Fahrenheit, specialized refrigeration systems, and enhanced power capacity — cannot be built at conventional warehouse prices, which creates a structural scarcity premium that anchors pricing. Harwick Cold Chain Partners' development cost of $118 million in 2021 has been supplemented by construction cost inflation to produce a replacement cost that Trellis estimates is more than 30 percent above the $148.5 million acquisition price — a meaningful cushion for a specialty industrial buyer seeking downside protection. Dunford's Northhaven submarket vacancy of 1.8 percent for cold storage is among the tightest specialty industrial segments in any market. Galveston Life Insurance's $95 million fixed-rate mortgage at 5.55 percent reflects institutional comfort with the Meridian Fresh Logistics triple-net credit profile and the specialized collateral type. Westport Industrial Advisors' exclusive advisory generated a competitive process that cleared at a premium to conventional warehouse pricing.
restate_4: The Trellis Cold Storage Center acquisition was one of the more significant cold storage transactions in the Dunford market's recent history, and Trellis Commerce Trust's willingness to pay $275 per square foot — a 25.8 percent premium over the 2021 development cost — was subsequently validated by the ongoing appreciation of cold storage assets nationally. Harwick Cold Chain Partners had assembled a specialized facility that was essentially irreplaceable at its cost basis: by 2026, construction inflation and supply chain disruptions had pushed new cold storage development costs in comparable markets well above $300 per square foot. Meridian Fresh Logistics' 15-year triple-net lease, signed in conjunction with Harwick's 2021 development and carrying through 2036, provided Trellis with a long runway of predictable income at $8.4 million annually growing at 2 percent per year. The 5.65 percent going-in cap rate was consistent with institutional pricing for single-tenant, specialized triple-net assets with investment-grade-proximate logistics tenancy. The 1.8 percent Dunford cold storage vacancy rate continued to tighten through the year, confirming the scarcity dynamic that underpinned the acquisition thesis.

paragraph: Corning Realty Trust, a non-traded perpetual-life REIT focused on industrial and logistics real estate, acquired the Sycamore Industrial Collection, a portfolio of nine infill last-mile distribution facilities totaling 975,000 square feet in the urban core of Halborn, for $212 million, or $217.44 per square foot. The assets, ranging in size from 48,000 to 165,000 square feet, are located within five miles of the Halborn central business district and serve dense population clusters with estimated 30-minute delivery reach of 2.4 million households. The portfolio is 99 percent leased to 17 tenants, with a weighted average remaining lease term of 4.1 years and average in-place rents of $17.20 per square foot, estimated 11 percent below current market rents of approximately $19.25 per square foot for comparable infill product. The going-in cap rate was 5.15 percent. Corning Realty Trust financed the acquisition with a $130 million secured credit facility from Aldwick National Bank at SOFR plus 175 basis points. The seller, Halborn Infill Industrial Partners, an affiliate of Riverside Opportunity Advisors, had assembled the portfolio through off-market acquisitions from 2018 to 2023. Fernbrook Capital Markets advised the seller.

restate_1: Corning Realty Trust is excited to announce the acquisition of the Sycamore Industrial Collection — nine infill last-mile distribution facilities totaling 975,000 square feet in the urban core of Halborn — representing a defining addition to our industrial and logistics portfolio. At $217.44 per square foot, the nine assets' proximity to 2.4 million households within a 30-minute delivery radius positions this collection as one of the most strategically located last-mile platforms in the Halborn market. The portfolio's 99 percent lease rate across 17 tenants provides current income stability at $17.20 per square foot, estimated 11 percent below market — a gap that the 4.1-year weighted average remaining lease term will allow us to capture through mark-to-market renewals targeting $19.25 per square foot and above. Aldwick National Bank's $130 million credit facility at SOFR plus 175 basis points provides flexible, competitive financing. Halborn Infill Industrial Partners' disciplined off-market assembly from 2018 to 2023 created the platform that we are now positioned to optimize for our investors.
restate_2: Corning Realty Trust's $212 million acquisition of the Sycamore Industrial Collection raises questions about the near-term lease rollover dynamics relative to the 5.15 percent going-in cap rate. The 4.1-year weighted average remaining lease term on a nine-building, 17-tenant portfolio in a dense urban environment creates meaningful rollover exposure within the typical holding period for a perpetual-life non-traded REIT. The 11 percent below-market rents at $17.20 per square foot — versus $19.25 per square foot for comparable infill product — is an embedded rent-growth opportunity, but capturing that growth requires tenants to renew at above-current rents in a leasing environment where some urban last-mile tenants have been rationalizing their footprints in response to shifting e-commerce economics. The $130 million secured credit facility from Aldwick National Bank at SOFR plus 175 basis points is well-priced for an infill industrial portfolio, but at a floating rate it introduces cost sensitivity. At $217.44 per square foot, Corning is paying a meaningful premium to suburban industrial pricing that is justified by the last-mile location attributes but also represents a thinner margin of safety against near-term market rent softening.
restate_3: Corning Realty Trust's acquisition of the Sycamore Industrial Collection reflects the institutional premium that infill last-mile distribution real estate commands in supply-constrained urban markets. At $217.44 per square foot for nine assets within five miles of the Halborn CBD, the pricing reflects both the scarcity of urban industrial sites available in any configuration and the operational premium that 30-minute delivery reach to 2.4 million households commands from logistics tenants. The 11 percent below-market rent — $17.20 per square foot in-place versus $19.25 per square foot market — is a classic value-add opportunity embedded in a near-stabilized portfolio: 17 tenants across 99 percent occupancy provide current income, while the 4.1-year lease term creates a defined mark-to-market window. Halborn Infill Industrial Partners' off-market assembly from 2018 to 2023 required patience and relationship capital that built barriers to entry for competing buyers. Aldwick National Bank's $130 million facility at SOFR plus 175 basis points is competitive pricing for the collateral quality. Fernbrook Capital Markets generated the institutional transaction process that achieved the 5.15 percent cap rate outcome.
restate_4: The Sycamore Industrial Collection represented Halborn Infill Industrial Partners' most significant capital realization since the affiliate of Riverside Opportunity Advisors began assembling the nine-building portfolio through off-market acquisitions starting in 2018. The $212 million sale to Corning Realty Trust at $217.44 per square foot validated the thesis that infill last-mile assets within five miles of a dense urban core — in this case, the Halborn CBD with 2.4 million households within 30 minutes — would attract institutional capital willing to accept sub-6 percent cap rates for the scarcity and demand durability of the product. Corning Realty Trust's mark-to-market strategy proved well-founded: over the four years following acquisition, 13 of the 17 tenants renewed at rents between $19.00 and $21.50 per square foot, reflecting sustained demand for infill last-mile space in Halborn's urban core. The weighted average in-place rent grew from $17.20 to $19.85 per square foot by the fourth year of ownership, generating NOI growth that exceeded the acquisition underwriting and confirmed the 5.15 percent going-in cap rate as appropriately priced for the asset quality and location.

paragraph: Brightfield Property Group successfully completed the development and full lease-up of the Brightfield Tech Quarter, a 520,000-square-foot Class A office and research campus in the Millhaven Innovation District, achieving 100 percent occupancy two months ahead of schedule. The five-story campus, constructed over 28 months at a total cost of $218 million, or $419 per square foot, includes 480,000 square feet of office space and 40,000 square feet of amenity and retail uses. Anchor tenants include Vantage Analytics Corporation, which signed a 12-year lease for 180,000 square feet in 2023 prior to groundbreaking, and Westbridge Biotech Holdings, which executed a 10-year lease for 115,000 square feet in 2024. The remaining tenancy is distributed among six additional firms in technology, professional services, and financial services. Effective rents average $39.50 per square foot on a full-service basis. Based on estimated stabilized net operating income of $16.4 million, the project's yield on cost is 7.52 percent. Brightfield is marketing the campus for sale or recapitalization, with initial price guidance in the range of $310 to $330 million, implying a going-in cap rate of 5.0 to 5.3 percent. Greystone Property Markets is running the formal disposition process.

restate_1: Brightfield Property Group is delighted to announce the full lease-up of the Brightfield Tech Quarter in the Millhaven Innovation District — a landmark development achievement completed two months ahead of our original schedule and representing the most successful office and research campus delivered in this market in recent years. The 520,000-square-foot, five-story campus was constructed over 28 months at $218 million and is now home to eight tenants including Vantage Analytics Corporation at 180,000 square feet on a 12-year commitment and Westbridge Biotech Holdings at 115,000 square feet on a 10-year lease. With effective rents averaging $39.50 per square foot full-service and estimated stabilized NOI of $16.4 million, Brightfield Tech Quarter has achieved a 7.52 percent yield on our development investment. We are now advancing the formal disposition or recapitalization process through Greystone Property Markets, with initial guidance in the $310 to $330 million range. We look forward to a conclusion that rewards our investors and positions this outstanding campus for its next ownership chapter.
restate_2: Brightfield Property Group's Brightfield Tech Quarter disposition at $310 to $330 million guidance — a 5.0 to 5.3 percent going-in cap rate — requires buyers to underwrite a meaningful premium to the $218 million development cost. The development spread of 7.52 percent yield on cost is attractive, but the proposed exit implies buyers would pay $419 to $635 per square foot — with the upper range well above the $419 per square foot development cost — for a fully leased campus whose weighted average lease term is constrained by the mix of 12-year and 10-year anchor leases signed in 2023 and 2024 and shorter commitments from the six additional tenants. The 40,000-square-foot amenity and retail component's rent contribution is modest and presents operational complexity relative to conventional full-service office space. At $39.50 per square foot full-service in the Millhaven Innovation District, the rents are competitive but not extraordinary, and future rent growth will depend on the district's continued tenant demand. Greystone Property Markets' disposition process will test whether the 5.0 to 5.3 percent cap rate guidance clears against institutional buyer appetite for fully leased Class A office with anchor life sciences tenancy.
restate_3: Brightfield Property Group's achievement of full lease-up on the Brightfield Tech Quarter ahead of schedule is a significant milestone that validates the Millhaven Innovation District as a competitive destination for technology and life sciences office users. The 7.52 percent yield on cost from a $218 million, 520,000-square-foot development program is a strong development return, particularly given the two-months-ahead-of-schedule performance. The anchor tenant profile — Vantage Analytics Corporation at 180,000 square feet and Westbridge Biotech Holdings at 115,000 square feet — provides the long-duration commitment that institutional buyers require for Class A office acquisitions. At $39.50 per square foot full-service across a diversified eight-tenant roster, the rent basis is durable and below the market ceiling in the Millhaven Innovation District. Brightfield's $310 to $330 million price guidance implies a 5.0 to 5.3 percent cap rate — a range consistent with institutional pricing for fully leased, anchored Class A office and research product in established innovation corridors. Greystone Property Markets is well-positioned to generate competitive interest from domestic and international institutional buyers.
restate_4: The Brightfield Tech Quarter lease-up and subsequent disposition process became one of 2026's most closely tracked office and research campus transactions. Brightfield Property Group's execution — full lease-up at 100 percent occupancy two months ahead of the 28-month construction schedule, at $39.50 per square foot full-service effective rents — was the validation that the Millhaven Innovation District had been seeking after several years of market development investment. Greystone Property Markets ran a formal process that attracted interest from both domestic institutional investors and international sovereign-adjacent capital. The campus was ultimately acquired at $318 million — within the guidance range — at a 5.16 percent going-in cap rate. Brightfield's development spread of approximately 230 basis points between yield on cost and exit cap rate represented one of the better office development returns achieved in the Millhaven market during the expansion cycle.

paragraph: Halcrest Realty Partners, acting through its Halcrest Stabilized Income Fund II, acquired a portfolio of three Class B multifamily communities totaling 742 units in the suburban Westport and Ferndale neighborhoods of Kellerton for $97.6 million, or $131,537 per unit. The seller, a private family partnership, had owned the communities for between 18 and 24 years. At closing, portfolio-wide occupancy was 93.2 percent, with average in-place rents of $1,105 per month, estimated at 16 percent below market. The three properties range from 196 to 312 units and were originally constructed between 1984 and 1992. Halcrest's renovation plan targets 85 percent of units for kitchen, bathroom, and flooring upgrades, with budgeted costs of $9,800 per unit and expected rent premiums of $145 to $190 per month post-renovation. Total renovation budget is $6.2 million over a 24-month period. The going-in cap rate was 6.2 percent on in-place income. Financing was secured from Windshore Federal Savings in the form of two fixed-rate agency-style loans totaling $63 million at a blended rate of 5.41 percent, with 10-year terms and 30-year amortization. Kellerton Residential Advisors brokered the sale process.

Portfolio occupancy at closing was 93.2 percent, with average in-place rents of $1,105 per month, estimated 16 percent below market. The going-in cap rate was 6.2 percent. Halcrest plans $9,800 per unit upgrades targeting 85 percent of units, with projected rent premiums of $145 to $190 per month. Total renovation budget is $6.2 million over 24 months. Windshore Federal Savings provided two fixed-rate loans totaling $63 million at a blended 5.41 percent. Kellerton Residential Advisors handled the sale.
restate_1: Halcrest Realty Partners, through Halcrest Stabilized Income Fund II, is pleased to announce the acquisition of three suburban Class B multifamily communities in Kellerton — a market we have studied closely and believe offers exceptional risk-adjusted value-add returns from a long-tenured family seller. The 742-unit portfolio, acquired at $131,537 per unit and a 6.2 percent going-in cap rate, reflects the below-market rent opportunity that makes value-add multifamily so compelling: with average in-place rents of $1,105 per month estimated at 16 percent below market, our renovation program targets meaningful income growth. We will invest $9,800 per unit in kitchen, bathroom, and flooring upgrades on 85 percent of the portfolio, with expected rent premiums of $145 to $190 per month post-renovation — generating $6.2 million in total renovation investment over 24 months. Windshore Federal Savings' $63 million in fixed-rate financing at a blended 5.41 percent provides a stable, long-term capital foundation. We thank Kellerton Residential Advisors for their professional execution of the sale process.
restate_2: Halcrest Realty Partners' $97.6 million Kellerton multifamily acquisition at $131,537 per unit and a 6.2 percent going-in cap rate on a three-property Class B portfolio offers a value-add thesis that is straightforward but requires execution discipline. The 16 percent below-market rent gap — from $1,105 in-place to an implied $1,316 per month market rate — is the core underwriting assumption, and the $9,800 per unit renovation program is intended to justify the premium. However, the portfolio's vintage — 1984 to 1992 — raises questions about whether the $9,800 per unit budget captures the full scope of upgrades needed for kitchen, bathroom, and flooring improvements in 35-to-40-year-old apartments, particularly in buildings that have been family-owned for 18 to 24 years without institutional capital management. The $145 to $190 per month post-renovation premium target is achievable in a healthy Kellerton apartment market, but depends on sustained market rent growth in the Westport and Ferndale neighborhoods through the 24-month renovation cycle. Windshore Federal Savings' fixed-rate loans at 5.41 percent provide rate certainty, which is the most conservative element of the financing structure.
restate_3: Halcrest Realty Partners' three-property Kellerton acquisition exemplifies the value-add multifamily strategy that has proven most durable across market cycles: below-market rents in well-located suburban communities with long-tenured family ownership and deferred but manageable capital needs. The 6.2 percent going-in cap rate on 93.2 percent occupancy provides a solid current income foundation, while the 16 percent below-market rents offer a defined mark-to-market pathway through the 24-month renovation program. The $9,800 per unit renovation budget — applied to 85 percent of the 742-unit portfolio — targets kitchens, bathrooms, and flooring: the improvements with the clearest causal relationship to rent premiums of $145 to $190 per month. Windshore Federal Savings' two fixed-rate agency-style loans totaling $63 million at a blended 5.41 percent provide long-term cost certainty that supports the fund's income distribution targets. The private family partnership's 18-to-24-year hold validated the Kellerton market's stability; Halcrest is now positioned to extract the value-add upside through a disciplined renovation and re-leasing program.
restate_4: The three-community Kellerton portfolio acquisition by Halcrest Realty Partners marked a typical but well-structured value-add transaction in the suburban multifamily segment during 2026. The private family partnership that had owned the assets for 18 to 24 years had managed the communities conservatively — 93.2 percent occupancy was solid, but the 16 percent below-market rents at $1,105 per month reflected years of below-market rent increases that preserved tenant relationships at the cost of income growth. Halcrest's $9,800 per unit renovation program, applied to 85 percent of the 742 units over 24 months, was completed on budget. Rent premiums averaged $162 per month on renovated units — within the $145 to $190 target range — as the Westport and Ferndale submarkets continued to absorb the upgraded product. The two Windshore Federal Savings fixed-rate loans at a blended 5.41 percent remained cost-effective through the hold period, providing predictable debt service against a growing NOI base. The portfolio's occupancy stabilized at 95.7 percent by the end of the renovation cycle, above the 93.2 percent starting point, confirming the product-market fit of the renovation program.

paragraph: Sageworth Data Ventures announced the execution of a 20-year ground lease with the Alderton Port Authority on a 22-acre industrial waterfront parcel at 1 Harbor Commerce Drive in the Portside submarket of Alderton, enabling the development of a 150,000-square-foot edge computing and carrier-neutral data center facility with an aggregate power capacity of 40 megawatts. Annual ground rent is $2.1 million, subject to CPI adjustments every five years capped at 3 percent. Total development cost is estimated at $310 million, inclusive of $180 million in data center-specific mechanical and electrical infrastructure. Sageworth has secured pre-commitment agreements from four network operators and two cloud service providers covering 28 megawatts of critical load capacity at a blended rate of $210 per kilowatt per month on 10-year contracts. The project is being financed with a $195 million construction loan from Crestmont Infrastructure Lending and $115 million in equity from Sageworth's flagship digital infrastructure fund. Construction is expected to begin in August 2026, with Phase I delivery of 20 megawatts targeted in the fourth quarter of 2027. Duncastle Real Estate Advisors advised the Port Authority on the ground lease negotiation.
